discord.title: Where the latest MCPs live
discord.description: On Discord, newly released MCP servers are shared all the time, and the community keeps posting automation use-cases and tips built with MCP. Catch the latest first and grow your setup together with everyone.

chat.msg1: Found a new Notion MCP — looks super useful 👇
chat.msg2: Built an auto-summarizer for Slack meeting notes with it 🔥
chat.msg3: Pinned this week's roundup of newly released MCPs 📌

home.title: MCP Hosting - Nodeflare
home.heroDescription1: Just hand us your GitHub MCP repo—we auto-convert your stdio server into an SSE-ready endpoint.
home.heroDescription2: OAuth 2.0 auth is built in, so you can go live in minutes with zero infrastructure to manage.

features.title: Everything you need to run MCPs in production, all in one place.

zeroConfig.title: stdio→SSE Auto-Convert
zeroConfig.desc: Auto-convert any stdio MCP from GitHub to SSE by just specifying the URL. No fork needed, deploy instantly.

acl.title: Fine-Grained Permissions
acl.desc: Control which tools each access token can call at the method level. OAuth supported.

secrets.title: Secret Management
secrets.desc: Store environment variables encrypted. Share safely with your team.

protocol.title: Audit with Access Logs
protocol.desc: Record who called what and when. Complete access logs only possible with SSE format.

alwaysOn.title: Prevent Internal MCP Leakage
alwaysOn.desc: With stdio, you can't track who called what. SSE format logs every token, enabling instant detection of external leaks.

devExperience.title:
No Need to
Implement Auth
devExperience.description: No auth code needed in your MCP server. Nodeflare handles token authentication, per-tool permissions, and audit logging for you.
devExperience.descriptionExtra: Bearer Token and OAuth 2.0 are attached automatically, and you can allow or deny tools/call on a per-method basis. Every request is recorded in audit logs, so you can always trace who called which tool and when—all without changing a single line of code in your MCP.
devExperience.noCode: Zero code changes to your MCP

bareMetal.title: Cheaper and Faster Than Anywhere Else
bareMetal.description: From August 2026, we're moving to dedicated bare-metal servers. By leaving shared environments behind and owning the full resources, we deliver hosting that's cheaper and faster than anywhere else.
bareMetal.descriptionExtra: Physical servers with zero overhead mean no compromise on latency or price—making production MCP hosting smoother than ever.

pricing.title: Most Features Are Free
pricing.subtitle: Free tier is enough for personal use. Go Pro for production.

free.name: Free
free.description: Perfect for personal projects and testing

q1.question: What is Nodeflare?
q1.answer: A dedicated hosting service for MCP. Auto-convert any stdio-format MCP server from GitHub to SSE format and deploy. Fine-grained access token permissions and OAuth supported. Most features are free.

q2.question: What's the difference between stdio and SSE?
q2.answer: stdio is for local execution and doesn't log who called what or when. SSE format accesses via HTTP, so all requests are logged and per-token permission control is possible. Effective for preventing internal MCP leakage.

q3.question: What are the benefits for enterprises?
q3.answer: With SSE format, 'who called which tool and when' is fully logged. Audit logs impossible with stdio enable instant detection and response if internal MCPs leak externally.

accessMode.title: Access Mode
accessMode.description: Control how this server can be accessed

authEnabled.title: NodeFlare Authentication
authEnabled.description: When disabled, NodeFlare's authentication layer is skipped. Use this when your MCP server handles its own authentication.

toolFilter.title: Scope-Based Tool Filtering
toolFilter.description: When enabled, tools/list only shows tools the calling credential is allowed to call, reducing the tokens an AI client loads upfront. Call-time permission checks always apply regardless.

toolSlim.title: Trim Tool Schemas
toolSlim.description: When enabled, long tool descriptions in tools/list are truncated to save tokens. Off by default because some clients rely on full descriptions.

toolSearch.title: Search-First Tools
toolSearch.description: When enabled, tools/list exposes only two meta-tools — search_tools and call_tool — so the AI client discovers tools on demand instead of loading every schema upfront. Keeps token cost roughly constant no matter how many tools the server has. Off by default because it changes the tool surface clients see.

toolCode.title: Code Execution
toolCode.description: When enabled, the AI writes JavaScript against your tools and the proxy runs it in a locked-down sandbox, returning only the final result. Cuts tokens for multi-step tasks by keeping intermediate data out of the model. Requires a configured code runner; otherwise this has no effect. Off by default.

upgrade.deployLimit: You've reached your deployment limit for this month. Upgrade to continue deploying.
upgrade.memoryLimit: Larger memory sizes require a higher plan.

create.title: Create New Server
create.welcomeDesc: Deploy MCP servers in seconds. Just connect your GitHub repository and we'll handle the rest.
create.name: Server Name
create.slugHelp: URL identifier (lowercase letters and hyphens only)
create.publicUrlHelp: Deploy any public GitHub repository directly. No fork required.
create.aiPromptHelp: Copy a prompt and paste it into an AI (e.g. ChatGPT or Claude). Even if you cannot read code, an AI that reads the repository can tell you what to put in each field below.
create.aiPromptTemplate:
I want to deploy the following MCP server to a hosting service called NodeFlare. I cannot read code myself, so please look at the repository and tell me what values to enter in the server creation form.

Repository: {repo}
Branch: {branch}

[Output format] Output ONLY the template below, one item per line as "Label: value". Do not write any intro, explanation, notes, or code blocks — just the values. Give exactly one value per item, with no alternatives or caveats.

Runtime: (exactly one of Node.js / Python / Go / Rust / Docker, using this spelling)
Transport: (either Streamable HTTP or STDIO)
Root directory: (path of the subdirectory containing the server code, or "none" if it is at the repo root)
Build command: (the command needed to build, or "none")
Start command: (the command that starts the server only)
MCP path: (a path like /mcp)
Listening port: (port number only, or "unknown")
Environment variables: (each as KEY=purpose, separated by ", "; key names use uppercase letters and underscores only; or "none")

[Rules]
- Do NOT include build steps in the start command (e.g. "npm run build && npm start" is not allowed; use only the start part like "npm start").
- Do NOT use npx in the start command.
- For Runtime and Transport, use exactly the spellings listed above.
- If something cannot be determined from the repository, write "unknown".
create.envVarsHelp: Set environment variables (secrets) now so they're available on the first deploy. Values are stored encrypted. You can change them later from the server's Secrets tab.
create.rootDirectoryHelp: For monorepos, specify the subdirectory where the MCP server code is located
create.mcpPathHelp: HTTP path where the MCP server listens (e.g., /mcp, /sse, /)
create.mcpPathAutoStdio: Automatically set to /mcp for STDIO transport
create.portHelp: Internal port your HTTP server listens on. Leave empty to use the runtime default (read from the PORT env var). Set this only if your server hardcodes a different port.
create.entryCommandHelp: Custom command to start the server (e.g., python server.py, uv run mcp-server). If empty, auto-detected based on project structure.
create.buildCommandHelp: Custom command to build the server before starting (e.g., npm run build, npm run compile). If empty, Node projects run their 'build' npm script automatically when present.
create.machineMemoryHelp: RAM for the server's machine. Browser-based servers (Playwright, Puppeteer) typically need 2 GB. If too small, the server may run out of memory and fail to start.
create.authEnabledHelp: When disabled, NodeFlare's authentication layer is skipped. Use this when your MCP server handles its own authentication (e.g., Notion API).
create.authDisabledWarning: Authentication is disabled. Make sure your MCP server handles authentication itself.
create.transportHelp: How the MCP server communicates (Streamable HTTP for servers already served over HTTP, STDIO for stdin/stdout servers that are auto-converted)
create.transportSseDesc: Already serves MCP over HTTP (proxied as-is; legacy SSE also works)
create.regionHelp: Select the region to deploy your server

faqPage.title: FAQ
faqPage.subtitle: Frequently asked questions about Nodeflare.
faqPage.notFound: Can't find what you're looking for? Feel free to

basic.a1: Nodeflare is a cloud hosting service for MCP servers. MCP servers allow AI assistants like Claude to access external tools and data. Simply connect your GitHub repository and builds and deployments happen automatically.
basic.a2: An MCP (Model Context Protocol) server defines tools that AI assistants can use. For example, you can provide database queries, file operations, external API calls, and any other functionality to AI.
basic.q3: Which AI can I use this with?
basic.a3: Currently, we support Anthropic's Claude. You can use it with Claude Desktop, Claude.ai, or via API — any MCP-compatible environment.

pricing.q1: What can I do with the free plan?
pricing.a1: The free plan includes up to 3 servers and 10,000 requests per month. This is sufficient for personal use and small projects.
pricing.q2: When should I upgrade to a paid plan?
pricing.a2: Consider upgrading to Pro (¥2,980/month) if you need more than 4 servers, exceed 10,000 monthly requests, or require custom domains and priority support.
pricing.a3: We accept credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, American Express, JCB). Contact us if you need invoice billing.

technical.a1: We support TypeScript/JavaScript, Python, Go, and Rust. Custom containers using Dockerfile are also available.
technical.a2: Typically, deployment completes within 1-2 minutes after pushing to GitHub. The first build may take slightly longer due to dependency installation.
technical.q3: How do I set environment variables?
technical.a3: You can securely set environment variables from each server's settings page in the dashboard. Sensitive information like API keys and secrets are stored encrypted.
technical.a4: Custom domains are available on Pro plan and above. SSL certificates are automatically issued after DNS configuration.

security.a1: You can issue access tokens for each server. Permissions can be set per token, and tokens can be revoked anytime when no longer needed.
security.a2: Yes, you can view logs for all tool invocations from the dashboard. This includes request content, responses, and execution times.
security.a3: Servers run in the Tokyo region, and data is stored domestically in Japan.

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.subtitle: Free for small projects. Scale as you grow.
pricing.enterpriseCustomPricing: Custom pricing based on your needs

faq.title: FAQ
faq.q1: Is it easy to upgrade from the free plan?
faq.a1: Yes, you can upgrade with one click from your dashboard. Your data and settings will be preserved.
faq.a2: We accept credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, American Express, JCB).
faq.a3: Yes, you can cancel anytime. You'll continue to have access until the end of your billing period.
faq.q4: What happens if I exceed my request limit?
faq.a4: We'll notify you as you approach your limit. If exceeded, additional charges may apply or requests may be temporarily throttled.
faq.q5: How much do I save with yearly billing?
faq.a5: You save about 17% with yearly billing. For example, the Pro plan is ¥2,980/month, but with yearly billing it's about ¥2,483/month equivalent.

docs.title: How NodeFlare works
docs.subtitle: The technical internals of the builder, the stdio→HTTP adapter, the proxy, and the sandboxed code runner — the pieces that make NodeFlare an MCP-native platform, not a generic container host.

contact.prompt: Questions about the internals, or want something that is not covered here?

overview.title: Built for MCP, not generic containers
p: NodeFlare is a hosting platform specialized for Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Every layer — how we build your repo, how we expose it, how we route requests — is aware of MCP semantics: the JSON-RPC methods (initialize, tools/list, tools/call, resources/*, prompts/*), MCP sessions, and per-tool / per-resource authorization.

A generic PaaS hands you a container and a port. NodeFlare instead takes an MCP server — even one that only speaks stdin/stdout — and turns it into an authenticated, scope-controlled, token-optimized Streamable HTTP endpoint that any MCP client can reach by URL.

This page documents the actual internals: the Rust builder, the injected Node stdio adapter, the Rust proxy, and the Deno sandbox that runs AI-written code. Nothing here is marketing abstraction — it maps directly to the code that runs your server.

architecture.title: Architecture at a glance
p: Three components do the work. The Builder turns a GitHub repository into a running Fly.io machine. The stdio adapter (injected at build time) wraps a stdin/stdout MCP server and speaks HTTP on its behalf. The Proxy sits in front of every server and handles auth, scope, caching, session affinity, quota, and MCP-specific response transforms. A fourth, optional component — the Runner — executes AI-written code in a locked-down sandbox.
architecture.caption: The proxy only ever talks HTTP to endpoint_url, so it is entirely language-agnostic. The stdio adapter is what bridges any-language MCP servers into that HTTP world.

builder.title: The Builder: from a GitHub repo to a live MCP
p: The builder is a Rust service that clones your repo at a specific commit, works out how to build and start it, generates a Dockerfile, builds the image remotely on Fly.io, deploys a machine, and then proves the MCP server actually answers.

steps.d: Clone via the GitHub App tarball (or public git). root_directory is resolved with path-traversal protection; monorepos (npm/yarn/pnpm workspaces) are detected so dependencies hoisted at the repo root still resolve.

node/python/go/rust/docker is inferred from manifests (package.json, pyproject.toml / requirements.txt / uv.lock, go.mod, Cargo.toml). Package manager (npm vs pnpm) and the start / build commands are auto-derived — scripts.start → main → bin for Node, [project.scripts] or server.py / main.py for Python, and so on. An explicit entry_command / build_command always wins.
A per-runtime Dockerfile is generated (or your own is validated and wrapped). For stdio servers, the stdio adapter is copied in and made the container entrypoint.
Environment variables are decrypted from the database and passed to Fly via stdin (never argv), and never appear in build logs — a redaction pass scrubs known token shapes.
The image is built remotely (flyctl deploy --remote-only) and a machine is started in the chosen region with the resolved memory and internal port.

A started machine is not proof of a working MCP. The builder POSTs a real MCP initialize (up to 8 attempts); if the child crash-loops behind the adapter, the deploy is failed and the actual server error is surfaced instead of a false success.

builder.note: That last step is the MCP-specific safety net: a wrong entry path leaves the adapter's HTTP up while your server is dead. NodeFlare probes initialize so that "deployed" means "actually answering MCP".

adapter.title: The stdio → Streamable HTTP adapter
p: Most MCP servers are written to run locally over stdin/stdout — the way Claude Desktop launches them with npx. That is exactly what makes them hard to host. NodeFlare's answer is a small Node adapter that is injected into your image at build time and becomes the container entrypoint.

The adapter spawns your MCP server as a child process and bridges its stdio JSON-RPC to a Streamable HTTP endpoint (plus the legacy 2024-11-05 HTTP+SSE for older clients). It is completely language-agnostic: it does not care whether the child is Node, Python, Go, or Rust — it only speaks JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout.

features.d: Every incoming request is rewritten to a private JSON-RPC id before it reaches the shared child, and the caller's original id is restored on the way out — so many clients can share one process without collisions.
The child is initialized on startup and the result cached, so deployment health is detectable and clients never hit "already initialized" errors.
Crashes are retried with exponential backoff (1s → 30s) up to a budget; once spent, /health returns 503 so the platform surfaces the failure instead of hiding a crash-loop.
Session ids are generated on initialize; the MCP path (default /mcp), host, and port are wired from build config so the proxy can reach it.

proxy.title: The Proxy: auth, scope & MCP-aware routing
p: Every request to a hosted server passes through the Rust proxy. It resolves the target server from the request subdomain, authenticates the caller, enforces authorization at the MCP-method level, and only then forwards to the upstream endpoint — applying MCP-aware transforms on the way back.

Because it understands the MCP protocol, the proxy can do things a plain reverse proxy cannot: authorize an individual tools/call by tool name, filter a tools/list per caller, and pin an MCP session to the machine that created it.

features.d: Bearer tokens are either mcp_* API keys (hashed, Redis-cached, with brute-force lockout) or OAuth access tokens validated against the DB. The proxy also serves the OAuth discovery metadata (RFC 8414 / 9728).
Before forwarding, the caller's scopes are checked against the exact MCP method and target — e.g. tools:call:get_weather. A denial returns a JSON-RPC error and never reaches your server.
initialize picks a machine and binds the returned Mcp-Session-Id to it in Redis; follow-up requests are pinned back to that machine, with stale bindings detected and healed.
Read-only list methods are cached and de-duplicated, but the cache key includes the caller's identity — so one credential's filtered list is never served to another.
Per-minute limits and per-workspace monthly quotas are enforced with atomic Redis scripts; the quota is incremented before forwarding to avoid overshoot.

tokens.title: MCP token-optimization features
p: Large tool catalogs waste context: a server exposing 60 tools can spend thousands of tokens on tools/list before the model does anything. NodeFlare ships four per-server switches that reshape the MCP surface to save tokens — all applied in the proxy, transparently, without touching your server code.

items.d: tools/list is filtered to the tools the caller's credential is actually scoped to call. Narrower list, identical schemas.
Tool descriptions longer than 500 characters are truncated (UTF-8 safe). Names and input schemas are untouched.

tools/list collapses to two meta-tools — search_tools(query) and call_tool(name, args). The model searches the catalog (lexical, or semantic when embeddings are configured) and calls tools on demand, so the upfront token cost is roughly constant regardless of catalog size.

Exposes run_code + search_tools. Instead of many tool round-trips, the model writes JavaScript that calls tools.* — executed in a sandbox (see below). Ideal for filtering and joining across many calls.

code.title: Code mode & the sandboxed runner
p: When tool_code_mode is on, the model can call run_code with a block of JavaScript. NodeFlare executes it in a dedicated Deno-on-Firecracker sandbox — a separate service from the proxy — with a network allow-list that permits exactly one destination: the proxy's tool-callback endpoint.

Inside the sandbox, tools.NAME(args) is injected as an async function. Each call POSTs back to the proxy carrying a per-execution bearer token; the runner never sees your credentials or the upstream URL.

steps.d: The proxy mints a one-off UUID token, stores the server id, target URL and scopes in Redis with a TTL of the run timeout + 10s, and dispatches the code to the runner.
A fresh Deno subprocess runs the code with no filesystem, no env, no subprocess — only outbound network to the callback endpoint. A wall-clock timeout SIGKILLs it.

steps.t: Re-check scope on every tool call
steps.d: The callback endpoint looks up the token, confirms it is bound to this server, and re-validates the scope (e.g. tools:call:X) before forwarding a real tools/call upstream — the sandbox wrapper is never the security boundary.
Tool errors are wrapped so the model can catch and retry; the final value is serialized and returned as the run_code result.

code.limits: Guardrails: a configurable wall-clock timeout (default 15s) and a max tool-call budget per run (default 50). If no runner is configured, code mode degrades gracefully and run_code reports itself unavailable.

security.title: Security model
p: Authorization is enforced by the proxy and the callback endpoint — never by the client, the sandbox wrapper, or your server.
points: Scopes are checked per request against the exact MCP method and target; denials return a JSON-RPC error before reaching your server.
Cache and request-coalescing keys include caller identity, so scope- or subscription-filtered results never leak across credentials.
Code-execution tokens are single-purpose: bound to one server, short-lived, and re-checked for scope on every tool call.
The code sandbox has no filesystem, no env, and a one-destination network allow-list; it holds only a per-run token, not your credentials.
Secrets are encrypted at rest, injected to the builder via stdin, and redacted from build logs.
A deploy is only "successful" after a real MCP initialize round-trips — a crash-looping server fails the deploy instead of silently 500-ing.

apiErrors.INVALID_SLUG: Slug can only contain lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens. It cannot start or end with a hyphen.
apiErrors.INVALID_REPOSITORY: Repository format is invalid. Please enter in 'username/repository' format (e.g., username/my-repo).
apiErrors.INVALID_GITHUB_REPO: GitHub repository format is invalid. Please enter in owner/repo format.
apiErrors.INVALID_RUNTIME: The selected runtime is invalid. Please choose from Node.js, Python, Go, or Rust.
apiErrors.INVALID_TRANSPORT: Transport type is invalid. Please choose SSE or STDIO.
apiErrors.INVALID_WORKSPACE: The specified workspace does not exist or you don't have access.
apiErrors.INVALID_MCP_REPOSITORY: This repository could not be recognized as an MCP server. Please check that MCP SDK dependencies are properly configured.
apiErrors.REPO_NOT_FOUND: The specified repository was not found. Please check if the repository exists and is accessible.
apiErrors.NOT_MCP_REPOSITORY: This repository does not appear to be an MCP server. Please check if MCP SDK is properly configured.
apiErrors.SERVER_LIMIT_REACHED: You have reached your plan's server limit. Please upgrade to add more servers.
apiErrors.DEPLOYMENT_LIMIT_REACHED: You have reached this month's deployment limit. Please upgrade to continue deploying.
apiErrors.MEMORY_LIMIT_REACHED: The selected memory size exceeds your plan's limit. Please upgrade to use a larger machine.
apiErrors.INVALID_MEMORY: The selected memory size is not allowed. Choose 256MB, 512MB, 1GB, or 2GB.
apiErrors.SERVER_NOT_RUNNING: The server is not running. Please deploy it first.
apiErrors.NO_ENDPOINT: The server endpoint is not yet configured. Please wait for deployment to complete.
apiErrors.VALIDATION_ERROR: There are errors in your input. Please check and try again.
apiErrors.INTERNAL_ERROR: An error occurred while processing. Please check your input and try again.
apiErrors.NOT_FOUND: The requested resource was not found.
apiErrors.FORBIDDEN: You don't have permission to perform this action.
apiErrors.UNAUTHORIZED: Login is required. Please log in again.
apiErrors.USER_NOT_REGISTERED: This email is not registered. The user must sign up with GitHub first.
apiErrors.default: An error occurred. Please try again.

github.settingsDescription: Connect GitHub accounts to access your private repositories. You can link multiple accounts and switch between them when creating servers.
github.confirmUnlink: Are you sure you want to unlink {username}? You will no longer be able to access their repositories.
github.noAccountsDescription: Connect a GitHub account to access your private repositories when creating MCP servers.
github.helpItem1: You can link multiple GitHub accounts to access repositories from different accounts.
github.helpItem2: The primary account is used by default when creating new servers.
github.helpItem3: Each linked account has its own access token with repository permissions.

companyPage.title: Company Profile
companyPage.intro: This page provides our company profile and electronic public notices.
companyPage.name: Company Name
companyPage.addressValue: Sity Palace Kamitoyooka 210, 180-7 Kamitoyooka-machi, Takasaki-shi, Gunma, Japan
companyPage.businessValue: Hosting of MCP servers and provision of related services
companyPage.publicNoticeValue: Public notices shall be given electronically and posted on the Company website (https://nodeflare.tech/company). If electronic notice cannot be given due to an accident or other unavoidable reason, notice shall be given in the Official Gazette (Kanpo).
companyPage.noticeDesc: Public notices required under the Companies Act and other laws are posted on this page.
companyPage.noticeEmpty: There are currently no public notices posted.
